content_preview.title: JWST Detects Methane in Atmosphere of TOI-199b, the First Temperate Giant Exoplanet to Have Its Chemistry Mapped
content_preview.summary: NASA's James Webb Space Telescope has characterized the atmosphere of TOI-199b, a Saturn-sized world 330 light-years away—the first temperate giant exoplanet studied in detail.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers using NASA's James Webb Space Telescope have mapped the atmospheric chemistry of TOI-199b, a Saturn-sized planet orbiting its star more than 330 light-years from Earth, in a study published May 20, 2026 in [*The Astronomical Journal*](https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.3847/1538-3881/ae4fba). The findings mark the first time scientists have studied the atmosphere of a temperate giant exoplanet in detail, according to the research team from Penn State University and NA...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article with clear sections (Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know, Analysis). Appropriate technical depth for a science news piece. Word count (849) is within the 400-1200 range for the News category. No AI self-reference. Tone is neutral and professional throughout.
editor_notes.source_verification: 4 sources cited. Source 0 (Penn State press release, source-0.html.gz, HTTP 200): verified. Confirms the headline claim, planet details (TOI-199b, 330 light-years, ~175°F, ~100-day orbit, Saturn-sized), methane detection, team (Hu/Bello-Arufe), methodology (transmission spectroscopy, ~20h observations, 7h transit), and quotes attributed to Hu and Bello-Arufe. All quotes verified verbatim against the Penn State snapshot. Source 2 (ScienceDaily, source-2.html.gz, HTTP 200): verified. Corroborates all key facts and also confirms the journal citation (The Astronomical Journal, 2026; 171(6): 354, DOI 10.3847/1538-3881/ae4fba). Source 1 (phys.org, HTTP 403 bot-blocked): snapshot failed, file=null. The article attributes two Bello-Arufe quotes and one Hu quote to Phys.org. These quotes are verbatim-identical to quotes in the Penn State press release (source-0.html.gz), so the underlying attribution is sound — the quotes appear to originate from the Penn State release and were republished by Phys.org. The article could have (and arguably should have) cited Penn State directly for these quotes. Source 3 (IOP Science paper, source-3.html.gz): snapshot is a Radware bot-manager CAPTCHA page with no article content. The paper's highly specific technical details — G9V host star classification, radius 0.810 ± 0.005 Jupiter radii, mass 0.17 ± 0.02 Jupiter masses, Bayes factor ~700, C/H ratio ~13x solar metallicity, NIRSpec G395M instrument, December 23 2024 transit date, 4-5x spectral uncertainty inflation from pointing misalignment, 3-micron ammonia absorption feature, and transit timing variations suggesting outer non-transiting giant — could not be verified from any available snapshot. These details are attributed to the published paper and are internally consistent and plausible for an Astronomical Journal paper, but cannot be independently confirmed from the captured snapshots.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Core claims (methane detection, JWST, TOI-199b, Penn State/JPL team, transmission spectroscopy, temperate giant novelty) are fully supported by the Penn State and ScienceDaily snapshots. Hu's institutional affiliation as 'associate professor of astronomy and astrophysics at Penn State' is confirmed. Bello-Arufe as 'postdoctoral researcher at JPL and lead author' is confirmed. The paper-level technical specifics (radii, masses, Bayes factor, instrument, date, metallicity, timing variations, 3-micron feature) are attributed to the IOP paper but cannot be verified due to bot-blocking. These represent plausible paper-level details consistent with the press release's description but are filed as a corrections note given they are unverifiable from available snapshots.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism. Appropriate hedging on unresolved findings (ammonia 'possible presence', 'preliminary' composition constraints). No editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate coverage found. Earlier JWST exoplanet articles cover different targets (buckyballs, TOI-5205b, Epsilon Indi, rocky exoplanets). This is a distinct first-of-kind detection.
concerns: The IOP Science paper snapshot (source-3) is a bot-manager CAPTCHA page containing no article content. Paper-specific technical parameters (G9V stellar type, radii 0.810 ± 0.005 Rjup, mass 0.17 ± 0.02 Mjup, Bayes factor ~700, C/H ~13x solar, NIRSpec G395M, December 23 2024 transit, 4-5x uncertainty inflation, 3-micron ammonia feature, transit timing variations) cannot be confirmed from any captured snapshot.

Quotes attributed to Phys.org (source-1, HTTP 403) appear verbatim in the Penn State press release (source-0). The attribution to Phys.org is technically inaccurate for sourcing purposes, though the quotes themselves are accurate. This is a minor misattribution — the quotes are real and appear in source-0; only the secondary cite is wrong.
recommendations: A corrections note should document that paper-level technical specifics were attributed to the IOP source but could not be verified due to bot-blocking of the IOP snapshot.
Future submissions should prefer citing the primary press release directly rather than secondary republishers when the press release is available and contains the same quotes.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Solid, well-sourced science news article. The core story — first atmospheric characterization of a temperate giant exoplanet — is fully supported by two independent verified snapshots. The main issues are (1) paper-level technical specifics unverifiable due to IOP bot-blocking, and (2) minor Phys.org quote misattribution where the same quotes appear in the Penn State source. Both are body-level issues that can be covered by a corrections record. Verdict: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
